records.ety: [OE. space, F. espace, from L. spatium space; cf. Gr. spa^n to draw, to tear; perh. akin to E. span. Cf. Expatiate.]

defs: Extension, considered independently of anything which it may contain; that which makes extended objects conceivable and possible.
quotes: Pure space is capable neither of resistance nor motion.

defs: Place, having more or less extension; room.
quotes: They gave him chase, and hunted him as hare; Long had he no space to dwell [in].
While I have time and space.

defs: A quantity or portion of extension; distance from one thing to another; an interval between any two or more objects; as, the space between two stars or two hills; the sound was heard for the space of a mile.
quotes: Put a space betwixt drove and drove.

defs: Quantity of time; an interval between two points of time; duration; time.
quotes: Nine times the space that measures day and night.
God may defer his judgments for a time, and give a people a longer space of repentance.
This ilke [same] monk let old things pace, And held after the new world the space.

defs: A small piece of metal cast lower than a face type, so as not to receive the ink in printing, -- used to separate words or letters.
The distance or interval between words or letters in the lines, or between lines, as in books, on a computer screen, etc.
notes: ☞ Spaces are of different thicknesses to enable the compositor to arrange the words at equal distances from each other in the same line.

defs: One of the intervals, or open places, between the lines of the staff.
that portion of the universe outside the earth or its atmosphere; -- called also outer space.

records.ety: [Cf. OF. espacier, L. spatiari. See Space, n.]

defs: To walk; to rove; to roam.
quotes: And loved in forests wild to space.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Spaced (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Spacong (?).]

defs: To arrange or adjust the spaces in or between; as, to space words, lines, or letters.
